content.text: Prosody is the musical layer of speech: the patterns of pitch, duration, and loudness that communicate emphasis, emotion, sentence type, and rhythm. A TTS system that generates accurate phonemes but monotone prosody sounds robotic even when its voice quality is otherwise excellent. Prosody control gives you explicit handles on these suprasegmental features, letting you shift from automatic prediction toward intentional, speaker- or context-driven shaping.
Every utterance varies on three independent axes.

The fundamental frequency of vocal cord vibration, measured in Hz. Rising intonation signals a question; falling intonation closes a statement. Speakers typically range 85–255 Hz (male) or 165–355 Hz (female). The log-scaled pitch contour is the dominant cue for emotion and sentence type.

How many audio frames each phoneme occupies. Stressed syllables are lengthened; unstressed ones compressed. Controlling duration controls perceived speech rate. At the model level, duration is a count of output mel frames per phoneme input token.

The root-mean-square amplitude of each frame, reflecting how much power is in the signal. Emphasis and prosodic stress are partly realised through energy increase on a syllable. In mel-spectrogram terms, energy corresponds to the mean spectral magnitude across frequency bins.

All three interact: a stressed syllable is typically higher-pitched, longer, and louder than its unstressed neighbours. Good prosody control exposes each dimension independently so they can be tuned without inadvertently coupling.

FastSpeech 2 (Microsoft, 2021) made prosody control a first-class design goal by introducing the Variance Adaptor between the phoneme encoder and mel decoder. The adaptor houses three lightweight convolutional predictors — one per prosodic dimension — that run in parallel over the phoneme-level hidden sequence.

attrs.alt: FastSpeech 2 architecture: phoneme encoder feeds Variance Adaptor containing duration, pitch, and energy predictors, whose embeddings are summed and passed to the mel decoder, then a vocoder produces audio. Bottom panel shows inference-time user controls for speed, pitch shift, energy, style tokens, and prosody transfer.

content.text: The duration predictor is a two-layer 1D convolutional network that maps each phoneme hidden vector to a scalar log-duration. During training, phoneme-to-frame alignments are extracted from a pre-trained acoustic model (FastSpeech 2 uses an MFA forced-aligner over a Tacotron 2 attention map). The mean squared error loss on log-durations drives the predictor.
At inference time, the Length Regulator repeats each phoneme hidden vector by its predicted duration count, expanding the sequence from phoneme length to frame length. A single
makes it 40% faster. The floor clamp at 1 prevents a phoneme from being collapsed to zero frames. Practical range is 0.5–2.0 before quality degrades noticeably.
Pitch is extracted from training waveforms using a pitch tracker (dio/harvest in WORLD, or pyin). FastSpeech 2 works at frame-level resolution, producing a pitch contour

where T is the number of mel frames. The pitch predictor, another 2-layer conv network, predicts this contour directly. The predicted (or override) contour is converted to a continuous embedding via a single linear projection and added element-wise to the frame sequence.
FastSpeech 2 normalises pitch by the speaker mean and standard deviation, so the predictor output is in z-score space. Shifting by a fixed offset raises or lowers pitch uniformly. Pitch in semitones

The energy predictor follows the same architecture as the pitch predictor but targets frame-level L2 norm of the mel-spectrogram vector. A scalar multiplier at inference time shifts the amplitude contour, making the output louder or softer. Because energy is predicted after the length regulator, it operates at frame resolution aligned with the expanded sequence.

The frame-level F0 representation in FastSpeech 2 has a problem: F0 is zero during unvoiced frames (fricatives, stops), creating discontinuities that are hard to model as a smooth signal. ProsoSpeech and related work addresses this by decomposing the pitch contour using the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT), which separates the pitch into components at different temporal scales:
capture local microintonation — the frame-to-frame fluctuation within a syllable.
capture phrase-level intonation shape — the rise/fall across a sentence.

By predicting CWT coefficients rather than raw F0, the model avoids voiced/unvoiced boundary artifacts. The wavelet representation also makes it easy to blend the global contour from one utterance with local pitch variation from another, enabling more principled style transfer.

Wang et al. (Google, 2018) introduced Global Style Tokens as a learned, interpretable latent space for speaking style. During training, a reference encoder compresses a ground-truth mel-spectrogram into a fixed-size style embedding. A multi-head attention layer then computes a weighted combination of 10 learned style token embeddings, creating the final style vector that is added to all frames before decoding.

The token weights are the style's representation: at inference, you can manually dial in a weight vector without a reference audio clip. After training, tokens often specialise interpretably — one token may correspond to upward intonation, another to a breathy voice, another to rapid energetic speech — though this is not guaranteed.

# Conceptual GST weighting at inference (Tacotron 2 + GST)
import torch

# 10 style tokens, each a learned 256-dim vector
style_tokens = model.gst.style_tokens  # shape: [10, 256]

# Manually set weights (no reference audio needed)
# Token 3 = upward intonation, token 7 = breathy quality
weights = torch.zeros(10)
weights[3] = 0.7   # mostly question intonation
weights[7] = 0.3   # slight breathiness
weights = torch.softmax(weights, dim=0)  # normalise

# Compute weighted style embedding
style_embedding = (weights.unsqueeze(1) * style_tokens).sum(dim=0)  # [256]

# Inject into decoder at every frame
# model synthesises with this style applied globally

A reference encoder is a convolutional-recurrent network that compresses an arbitrary mel-spectrogram clip into a fixed-length prosody embedding. By encoding a target speaker's reference audio, the synthesiser adopts the prosody patterns of that speaker even if the text content differs entirely.
Prosody transfer has two distinct modes:

The reference clip's single embedding is broadcast to all output frames. The result captures average speaking rate, pitch register, and energy level but loses fine-grained word-level variation. Suitable when the goal is to match a speaker's characteristic style.

The reference pitch and duration contours are extracted, warped to match the target phoneme sequence using Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), normalised by speaker statistics, and fed directly as pitch and duration overrides. This reproduces the original prosodic phrasing rather than just the average style.

# Fine-grained prosody transfer using Coqui TTS
from TTS.api import TTS
import librosa
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import resample

tts = TTS(model_name="tts_models/en/ljspeech/fast_pitch")

# Extract reference pitch contour
ref_wav, sr = librosa.load("reference.wav", sr=22050)
ref_f0, voiced_flag, _ = librosa.pyin(
    ref_wav,
    fmin=librosa.note_to_hz('C2'),
    fmax=librosa.note_to_hz('C7'),
    frame_length=1024,
    hop_length=256
)

# Normalise by reference speaker mean/std
voiced = ref_f0[voiced_flag > 0.5]
ref_mean = np.log(voiced + 1e-9).mean()
ref_std  = np.log(voiced + 1e-9).std()

# Synthesise with pitch override (Coqui FastPitch API)
# The model accepts a per-frame f0 tensor as input_pitch
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.",
    file_path="output.wav",
    # pitch_transform shifts all predicted F0 by +2 semitones
    pitch_transform=lambda f0: f0 * 2 ** (2 / 12)
)
Most production TTS libraries expose prosody controls as simple function arguments. Here are the most common patterns using a FastSpeech 2 / FastPitch backbone:

from TTS.api import TTS

tts = TTS(model_name="tts_models/en/ljspeech/fast_pitch")

# 1. Speed control (0.5 = half speed, 2.0 = double speed)
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="Hello world.",
    file_path="slow.wav",
    speed=0.75          # 25% slower
)

# 2. Pitch shift in semitones (positive = higher)
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="Hello world.",
    file_path="high.wav",
    pitch_transform=lambda f0: f0 * 2 ** (4 / 12)  # +4 semitones
)

# 3. Energy multiplier
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="Hello world.",
    file_path="loud.wav",
    energy_transform=lambda e: e * 1.4   # 40% louder
)

# 4. Combining speed + pitch + energy
tts.tts_to_file(
    text="This is urgent.",
    file_path="urgent.wav",
    speed=1.2,
    pitch_transform=lambda f0: f0 * 2 ** (2 / 12),
    energy_transform=lambda e: e * 1.3
)
For SSML-aware engines (Azure Cognitive Services, Google Cloud TTS, Amazon Polly), prosody control is expressed declaratively in markup:

<speak version="1.0" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/10/synthesis">
  <voice name="en-US-JennyNeural">
    <!-- Rate: x-slow, slow, medium, fast, x-fast, or percent -->
    <prosody rate="slow" pitch="+10%" volume="+20%">
      Take your time reading this sentence.
    </prosody>

    <!-- Per-word emphasis -->
    <emphasis level="strong">Do not</emphasis> touch that button.

    <!-- Absolute pitch target in Hz -->
    <prosody pitch="150Hz">
      I'm speaking at a fixed pitch.
    </prosody>
  </voice>
</speak>
Match average style of a speaker

FastSpeech 2's duration predictor is a single convolutional module trained in isolation after forced alignment. This is fast but carries a ceiling on timing naturalness because the predictor is not jointly optimised with the mel output. Two alternatives address this:

used in VITS: learns phoneme-to-frame alignments jointly with the decoder using a differentiable dynamic programming pass. The model learns to time phonemes based on what the decoder needs — resulting in more natural rhythm.
(Tacotron 2, NaturalSpeech 3): use soft attention to align text and audio, producing highly natural timing at the cost of less explicit control and slower inference.

The tradeoff is explicit control vs. naturalness. When you need to specify timing precisely (audio editing, dubbing lip-sync), the FastSpeech 2 duration predictor with manual overrides is more practical. For unrestricted expressive synthesis, end-to-end learned alignment produces better rhythm.

To place emphasis on a word without reference audio, increase its phoneme duration by 20–40%, raise its energy by 1.2–1.4×, and add a slight pitch boost on the stressed vowel. Most FastSpeech 2 implementations allow per-phoneme duration multipliers alongside the global speed scalar:

import numpy as np

# Suppose phonemes are [D, AO, N, T, ' ', T, AH, CH, ' ', DH, AE, T]
# We want to emphasise 'DONT' (phonemes 0-3)
n_phonemes = 12
duration_multipliers = np.ones(n_phonemes)
duration_multipliers[0:4] = 1.35   # 35% longer for 'DONT'

# Pass to model that supports per-phoneme duration control
tts.synthesise(
    phonemes=["D", "AO", "N", "T", "SIL", "T", "AH", "CH", "SIL", "DH", "AE", "T"],
    duration_multipliers=duration_multipliers,
    energy_multipliers=np.where(duration_multipliers > 1, 1.3, 1.0)
)

English yes/no questions end with rising intonation. If the model does not produce this automatically from punctuation, you can inject it by overriding the final few frames of the F0 contour with an exponentially rising trajectory. For wh-questions (falling intonation), the reverse applies. The override is cleanest when applied in log-F0 space where the change is perceptually linear.

When doing prosody transfer across speakers with very different pitch ranges, normalise by speaker statistics before applying the transfer to avoid pitch overflow. The target speaker's pitch should be in the same z-score space as the training distribution:

are the log-F0 mean and standard deviation for the reference and target speakers respectively. Without normalisation, transferring female prosody to a male voice model often causes the F0 to exceed the model's training range, producing crackling artefacts.
Evaluating prosody quality is harder than evaluating voice quality because prosody is highly context-dependent. The main approaches are:
Pitch prediction error vs. ground truth
Requires paired reference; penalises valid alternatives
Pearson r between predicted and ground-truth phoneme durations
Does not capture naturalness of rhythm
Human rating of naturalness on 1-5 scale
Classifier accuracy on synthetic speech labelled by intended emotion
Depends on classifier quality; discrete emotion labels are coarse

When a TTS system supports multiple speakers, prosody control and speaker identity become intertwined. A speaker is defined not just by voice timbre but also by characteristic pitch range, speaking rate, and rhythmic phrasing. Multi-speaker models must disentangle these dimensions to enable cross-speaker style transfer.

Most multi-speaker FastSpeech 2 implementations condition the model on a speaker embedding derived from a d-vector or x-vector speaker verification network. The embedding is added to the phoneme encoder output before the Variance Adaptor, so all three prosody predictors are conditioned on speaker identity. This means that when you synthesise with speaker A's embedding, you get A's characteristic rhythm and pitch range automatically — you then apply additional prosody controls on top.

A subtle failure mode in multi-speaker prosody transfer is style leakage: if you encode a reference clip from speaker A and try to synthesise with speaker B's voice, the style embedding may also carry residual speaker-A identity information (because pitch range and timbre are correlated in the reference encoder's training data). Solutions include:

Train a speaker classifier on the style embedding with gradient reversal, forcing the style encoder to produce speaker-invariant representations. The style embedding then captures only speaking style, not speaker identity.

Extract F0 contour from the reference, normalise by reference speaker statistics, re-scale to target speaker statistics (using the log-F0 z-score formula above), and inject only the normalised contour. This transfers prosodic pattern without transferring pitch register.

Maintain separate style and speaker encoders, trained with a reconstruction loss that requires both to be present for correct output. At inference, freely mix any style embedding with any speaker embedding.

Discrete emotion conditioning takes the idea of GST tokens further by training with labelled emotional speech data (Happy, Sad, Angry, Neutral, Surprised). The model learns an emotion embedding table and conditions the entire decoder on the selected emotion. At inference, no reference audio is required — you simply pass an emotion label.

FastSpeech 2 emotion conditioning works by adding an emotion embedding to the phoneme encoder output alongside the speaker embedding. The Variance Adaptor then predicts prosody consistent with that emotion, producing the characteristic pitch range and energy profile of the target emotional state.

# Emotion-conditioned TTS (FastSpeech 2 with emotion labels)
# EmotiVoice and similar models expose an emotion_id parameter

emotions = {
    "neutral": 0,
    "happy": 1,
    "sad": 2,
    "angry": 3,
    "surprised": 4
}

# Synthesise the same sentence with different emotions
for emotion_name, emotion_id in emotions.items():
    tts.tts_to_file(
        text="I can't believe you did that.",
        speaker_id=0,
        emotion_id=emotion_id,
        file_path=f"output_{emotion_name}.wav"
    )
    print(f"Synthesised with emotion: {emotion_name}")

# Note: same text, same speaker, but prosody differs:
# 'happy'  → higher pitch, faster rate, more energy
# 'sad'    → lower pitch, slower rate, less energy
# 'angry'  → wide pitch range, faster rate, high energy

Emotion conditioning is not always robust across all emotions. Anger and joy often come out convincingly because they have strong, unambiguous prosodic signatures. Sadness and surprise are harder: sadness varies widely across cultures, and surprise is often indistinguishable from question intonation without additional context. Mixing approach: start with a discrete emotion label, then apply additional fine-grained pitch and rate adjustments to taste.
Prosody operates on three independent axes: F0 (pitch), duration, and energy. FastSpeech 2 exposes all three via the Variance Adaptor.
The Length Regulator converts phoneme durations to frame counts. Speed control is a global scalar applied before expansion.
Pitch is normalised by speaker statistics during training, so pitch transfer requires re-normalising to the target speaker range.
CWT pitch decomposition avoids voiced/unvoiced discontinuities and enables multi-scale style blending.
GST tokens give a reference-free style space; the reference encoder enables style transfer from any audio clip.
SSML provides a declarative interface for prosody control in cloud TTS APIs without requiring model internals access.
